edges.description: Parthenogenetic activation of the germ cell drives differentiation into mature somatic tissue of multiple germ layers within the cyst.
BMP15 loss and H-Ras/MAPK activation increase spontaneous parthenogenetic oocyte activation, the initiating event in ovarian teratoma formation.
edges.source: BMP15 / H-Ras-MAPK Dysregulation in Hereditary Ovarian Teratoma

edges.description: Continued accumulation of keratin, sebaceous material, and hair within the epithelial-lined cyst causes gradual enlargement of a mass lesion.
Mature tri-lineage tissue forming a unilocular cyst at the ovary is the defining ovarian dermoid cyst lesion.
Ectoderm entrapped along the CNS midline differentiates into a dermoid cyst within the cranium.
Ectoderm entrapped during nasal midline fusion forms a nasal dermoid sinus cyst.
Ectodermal differentiation at the ocular surface forms a limbal/epibulbar dermoid (choristoma).
Entrapped ectoderm differentiates in situ into the mature epidermis, dermis, and skin appendages that constitute the dermoid cyst.
edges.source: Aberrant Embryonic Midline Fusion and Ectodermal Entrapment

edges.description: An enlarging or complicated ovarian dermoid cyst produces pelvic pain.
A large ovarian dermoid presents as a palpable abdominal/pelvic mass.
An enlarging intracranial dermoid cyst causes headache through mass effect.
An enlarging intracranial dermoid cyst is prone to rupture, releasing lipid contents.
edges.target: Cyst Rupture and Chemical Aseptic Meningitis

edges.description: Long-standing ovarian mature cystic teratomas rarely undergo malignant transformation of the squamous lining.
Spillage of cyst lipid content into cerebrospinal fluid triggers a chemical aseptic meningitis.
edges.source: Cyst Rupture and Chemical Aseptic Meningitis

edges.description: The transformed squamous lining gives rise to an invasive squamous cell carcinoma.
Anti-NMDAR encephalitis associated with ovarian teratoma commonly produces neuropsychiatric features and seizures.

edges.target: BMP15 / H-Ras-MAPK Dysregulation in Hereditary Ovarian Teratoma

nodes.description: A large ovarian dermoid cyst can present as a palpable abdominal/pelvic mass.

evidence.snippet: The current plane magnetic resonance imaging showed a large pelvic-abdominal cystic tumor measuring 17 cm, consisting of fat, fluid-fluid level, and a hair ball.

nodes.description: For craniofacial, nasal, orbital, ocular-surface, and cutaneous dermoid cysts, the lesion arises developmentally rather than from a germ cell. Surface ectoderm becomes entrapped during fusion of the facial prominences and midline seam closure (the ectodermal-inclusion and embryonic closure-defect theories), producing an epithelial-lined cavity that contains epidermis, dermis, and adnexal structures. Nasal dermoids may retain a sinus tract and, in a subset, intracranial extension.
nodes.id: Aberrant Embryonic Midline Fusion and Ectodermal Entrapment

evidence.snippet: Aberrant midline fusion and ectodermal inclusion during embryogenesis represent the principal pathogenic theories

Developmental midline nasal masses including nasal dermoids (NDs), encephaloceles (EPHCs), and nasal glial heterotopias (NGHs) are a consequence of disrupted embryonal developmental processes in the frontonasal region.

nodes.description: In a rare hereditary, X-linked dominant ovarian (immature) teratoma, a germline BMP15 missense mutation (C262T; p.Arg88Cys) reduces secretion of mature BMP15 by ~85%. Loss of BMP15 oocyte growth-factor signaling is associated with abnormal activation of the H-Ras/MAPK pathway in oocytes, which markedly increases the rate of spontaneous parthenogenetic activation and predisposes to teratoma formation. This defines a molecular, germline-mediated route to the parthenogenetic origin seen in sporadic tumors.
nodes.id: BMP15 / H-Ras-MAPK Dysregulation in Hereditary Ovarian Teratoma

evidence.snippet: A rare missense germline mutation (C262T) in the first exon of the BMP15 gene was identified.
In vitro experiments on cell lines confirmed that the mutation caused an 84.7% reduction in the secretion of mature BMP15.
In the transgenic mouse model, the spontaneous parthenogenetic activation significantly increased in oocytes carrying the T allele.
Oocyte-specific RNA sequencing revealed that abnormal activation of the H-Ras/MAPK pathway might contribute to the development of OIT.
BMP15 was identified as a pathogenic gene for OIT which improved our understanding of the etiology of OIT and provided a potential biomarker for genetic screening of this disorder.

nodes.description: Squamous cell carcinoma arising by malignant transformation of an ovarian mature cystic teratoma (~1-3% of cases).

evidence.snippet: We diagnosed this tumor as squamous cell carcinoma originating from mature cystic teratoma of the left ovary.

nodes.description: Rupture of an intracranial dermoid cyst releases lipid-rich keratinaceous contents into the subarachnoid space, provoking a chemical (aseptic) inflammatory meningitis affecting the meninges and adjacent cortex. Rupture of an ovarian dermoid likewise causes a chemical (granulomatous) peritonitis.
nodes.id: Cyst Rupture and Chemical Aseptic Meningitis

nodes.description: Ovarian mature cystic teratoma originates from a single ovarian germ cell/oocyte that develops parthenogenetically (without fertilization). The tumor typically arises after completion of meiosis I with failure of meiosis II, producing a near-diploid genome that contains only maternal genetic material. This parthenogenetic origin explains the largely cytogenetically bland karyotype and the characteristic imprinting abnormalities of these tumors, distinguishing them from malignant germ cell tumors driven by isochromosome 12p and somatic mutation.

evidence.snippet: An immature teratoma is a germinal malignant tumor composed of three germ cell layers, occurring more frequently in young women.
Precursory germ cells of the ovary form the basis of GCT.
They are histologically classified into primitive GCT, teratomas, and monodermal and somatic-type tumours associated with dermoid cysts.

nodes.description: Intracranial dermoid cysts frequently present with headache.

evidence.snippet: Surgery is the only method of treatment in order to prevent local and intracranial inflammatory complications as well as distant deformities of the facial skeleton.

nodes.description: A congenital intracranial dermoid cyst, a rare benign CNS lesion that may present with headache, seizures, and signs of mass effect; rupture can cause chemical meningitis.

evidence.snippet: meticulous preoperative differential diagnostics is mandatory

nodes.description: A congenital ocular-surface (limbal/epibulbar) dermoid choristoma that may induce astigmatism and visual disturbance.

In approximately 1-3% of ovarian mature cystic teratomas, the mature squamous epithelial lining undergoes malignant transformation, most commonly to squamous cell carcinoma. This is a late, rare complication, more frequent in older (postmenopausal) women and large or long-standing lesions.

evidence.snippet: Malignant transformation is a rare complication of ovarian mature cystic teratoma that occurs in 1-3% of cases.
A histological examination revealed that part of the thickened cyst wall was lined by squamous cell carcinoma in situ.

nodes.description: Within the cyst, the activated germ cell differentiates into mature, well-differentiated somatic tissues, with ectodermal derivatives predominating: a wall lined by keratinizing squamous epithelium with numerous adnexal structures (hair follicles, sebaceous glands, occasional apocrine glands and teeth), surrounded by a dense, concentrically arranged collagen stroma. The cyst lumen accumulates lamellar keratin, hair, and sebaceous material. At craniofacial and cutaneous sites the equivalent histology arises instead from ectoderm entrapped during midline fusion.

evidence.snippet: follicular tumors are classified according to the differentiation pattern seen in the corresponding part of the normal hair follicle

nodes.description: Rupture of an intracranial dermoid cyst can cause chemical aseptic meningitis from release of lipid contents into the cerebrospinal fluid.

evidence.snippet: Surgery is the only method of treatment in order to prevent local and intracranial inflammatory complications

nodes.description: A congenital nasal dermoid sinus cyst, the most common congenital midline nasal mass; presents as a painless midline mass, sometimes with a sinus opening and visible hair, and carries a high risk of recurrent infection.

evidence.snippet: Pediatric nasal dermoid/sinus cysts (NDSC) are common congenital midline craniofacial lesions associated with embryonic tissue entrapment and abnormal midline fusion during embryogenesis
they can involve intracranial structures and predispose patients to recurrent infection, intracranial complications, and cosmetic or functional impairment

nodes.description: A mature cystic teratoma of the ovary, the prototypical and most common dermoid cyst; often an asymptomatic adnexal mass discovered incidentally.

evidence.snippet: They are histologically classified into primitive GCT, teratomas, and monodermal and somatic-type tumours associated with dermoid cysts.

nodes.description: Ovarian teratomas (including mature/dermoid teratomas containing neural tissue) are the commonest tumor associated with anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (anti-NMDAR) encephalitis, a paraneoplastic autoimmune syndrome. Neural tissue within the teratoma is thought to trigger an autoimmune response against NMDA receptors; tumor removal is a central component of treatment.

evidence.snippet: Anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (anti-NMDAR) encephalitis is the commonest GCT-associated paraneoplastic syndrome.
Where possible, ovarian-sparing surgery should be offered.

nodes.description: Larger or complicated ovarian dermoid cysts may present with pelvic pain.
Intracranial dermoid cysts may present with seizures, including acute symptomatic seizures following rupture; seizures also occur in teratoma-associated anti-NMDAR encephalitis.

evidence.snippet: Paraneoplastic syndromes occurring with germ-cell-tumors (GCTs) are rare but can be life-threatening.

nodes.description: The dermoid cyst is a slow-growing, chronic lesion that gradually enlarges and does not spontaneously resolve. Enlargement and the dependent position of the ovarian mass predispose to mechanical complications (ovarian torsion, rupture) and, at intracranial and craniofacial sites, mass effect and infection. Persistence over years also underlies the rare late malignant transformation.

evidence.snippet: Magnetic resonance imaging performed at another Department of Internal Medicine 10 years ago revealed a left ovarian teratoma measuring 8 cm
